back_translation: One day, while he was teaching the people in the temple courts and proclaiming the good news, the chief priests and the scribes, together with the elders, came up to him.
They said to him, "Please tell us by what authority you are doing these things. Or who is the one who gave this authority to you?"
He answered and said to them, "I also will ask you one question. Answer me:
The baptism of John—did it come from heaven or from men?"
They discussed it among themselves, saying, "If we say, 'From heaven,' he will ask, 'Why then did you not believe John?'
But if we say, 'From men,' all the people will stone us, because they are convinced that John was a prophet."
So they answered that they did not know where it came from.
Jesus therefore said to them, "Neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things."
Then he began to tell this parable to the people: "A certain man planted a vineyard, let it out to tenant farmers, and went away to a foreign land for a long time.

When the time came to gather the fruit, he sent a servant to those tenant farmers to receive a share of the fruit of the vineyard. But the tenant farmers beat the servant and sent him away empty-handed.
So he sent another servant, but they also beat that servant, caused him to receive shame, and sent him away empty-handed.
Then he sent a third, but they also wounded this one and drove him out.
The owner of the vineyard then said, 'What shall I do? I will send my beloved son. Perhaps he will respect my son.'
But when the tenant farmers saw the son, they consulted among themselves, saying, 'This one is the heir. Let us kill him, so that the inheritance may fall to us.'
So they drove him out of the vineyard and killed him. What will the owner of the vineyard do to those people?
He will come and destroy those tenant farmers, and then give the vineyard to others." When they heard this, they said, "May it never be so."
But Jesus looked at them and said, "Then what does this scripture mean: 'The stone that all the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone'?
Everyone who falls upon that stone will be shattered into pieces, but on whomever that stone falls, it will crush that person into dust."
The scribes and the chief priests sought to arrest him at that very time, but they feared the people, because they knew that he had told this parable against them.
So they kept watching him and sent spies who pretended to be righteous men, in order to catch him in his words, so that they could hand him over to the authority of the governor.
So the spies asked him, "Teacher, we know that you speak and teach correctly, that you do not show partiality to anyone, but teach the way of God according to the truth.
Is it right according to God's law or not for us to pay taxes to Caesar?"
But Jesus saw through their scheme and said to them,
"Bring a denarius coin to me to see. Whose image and inscription are on this coin?" They answered, "Caesar's."
So he said to them, "In that case, the things of Caesar render to Caesar, and the things of God render to God."
They could not catch him in his words before the people, and they were amazed at his answer and fell silent.
Then some of the Sadducees, who deny that there is a resurrection from the dead, came to him and asked,

"Teacher, Moses wrote and commanded us: 'If a man's elder brother dies and has a wife but no child, the younger brother of the deceased shall take that wife as his own wife and have a child to continue the family line for the elder brother.'
Suppose there were seven brothers. The first took a wife and died without children.
The second did likewise.
The third also took the wife, and all seven did the same—they died without children to continue the family line.
Afterwards, the woman also died.
So, in the resurrection, whose wife will she be among them? For all seven had taken her as wife."
Jesus said to them, "The sons of this age marry and are given in marriage,
but those who are considered worthy to have a share in the age to come and in the resurrection from the dead will neither marry nor be given in marriage.
For they will no longer die at all, because they are equal to the angels and are sons of God, being sons of the resurrection.
But concerning the dead being raised, even Moses himself showed this in the passage about the burning bush, when he called the Lord 'the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.'
He is not the God of the dead, but the God of the living, for to him all are alive."
Some of the scribes then answered, saying, "Teacher, you have answered well."
And they no longer dared to ask him anything else.
Then he said to them, "How is it said that the Christ is the son of David?
For David himself said in the book of Psalms: 'The Lord said to my Lord, "Sit at my right hand,
until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet."'
Therefore David calls him Lord. Then how can he be the son of David?"
While all the people were listening, he said to his disciples,
"Beware of the scribes carefully, because they love to walk around wearing long robes, love greetings in the marketplaces, love the chief seats in the synagogues, and love the chief seats at feasts.
They are ones who devour the houses of widows, and pray lengthily as a cover. These people will receive heavier punishment."
